title: Per capita plastic waste vs. GDP per capita
subtitle: Per capita plastic waste generation rate is measured in kilograms per person per day. GDP per capita is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.
caption:
Per capita plastic waste vs. GDP per capita, 2010

Per capita plastic waste generation rate is measured in kilograms per person per day. GDP per capita is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.

This scatter plot maps the relationship between national wealth and daily plastic waste generation for individual countries in 2010. The horizontal axis plots GDP per capita in international dollars on a logarithmic scale ranging from $1,000 to $100,000, while the vertical axis measures plastic waste in kilograms per person per day, spanning from 0 kg to 0.6 kg. Each circle represents a country, with its size corresponding to total population and its color indicating its continent. The data points show a visible positive association where higher GDP per capita coincides with higher per capita plastic waste generation. Countries in North America and Europe generally occupy the upper-right quadrant of the chart. The United States, represented by a large orange circle, sits near the $50,000 mark with a waste rate of roughly 0.33 kg per person per day. Germany and the Netherlands appear in the upper right with waste rates approaching 0.5 kg per person per day. Kuwait and Antigua and Barbuda show the highest waste generation rates on the chart, exceeding 0.6 kg per person per day. Conversely, nations in Africa and South Asia cluster in the lower-left section. India and Indonesia appear as large teal circles near the bottom, with waste rates below 0.1 kg per person per day and GDP levels around $5,000. The Democratic Republic of the Congo and Togo sit at the lowest end of both scales, with waste generation near 0.02 kg per person per day. Several middle-income nations like China, Brazil, and Turkey occupy the center of the distribution with waste rates between 0.1 kg and 0.2 kg per person per day. The overall pattern displays a gradient where plastic waste generation increases alongside economic output across the global sample.
